segments.s072-g1: پس is an opening connective meaning "then." آگاهی here means "news/word" and is the subject; آمد is simple past "came/reached." سوی functions as the preposition "to/toward," and شهریار is the recipient "the king." The clause literally reads "then news came to the king," i.e. "then word reached the king."
segments.s072-g2: که introduces a content clause "that..."; آمد is past "came." ز is the poetic form of از meaning "from," governing ره "road/path." زال names the famous hero Zal (a proper name), and سام سوار is an appositional/title phrase "Sam, the rider" (سوار qualifying the name). The whole clause: "that Sam the rider had come from Zal's road."
segments.s072-g3: پذیره شدندش is a verbal phrase meaning "they received him" (پذیره = reception/receiving; شدند + clitic -ش = they ... him), with the object clitic attached. همه "all" qualifies the following noun سرکشان, here referring to the chiefs/leading men (poetically rendered). The clause means "all the chiefs received him."
segments.s072-g4: که introduces a relative/explanatory clause "who/that." بودند is the past tense "they were." در پادشاهی نشان is an idiomatic phrase meaning "holding marks/office in the kingdom," i.e. "those who held rank in the realm." Note در + پادشاهی (in the kingdom) and نشان (mark, token, here 'rank').
segments.s072-g5: چو is the temporal conjunction "when." آمد is past "came/approached." به نزدیکی بارگاه = "to the vicinity of the palace/court" (به + نزدیکی = to the nearness of; بارگاه = royal court). The phrase sets the time/place: "when he came near the court."
segments.s072-g6: سبک here functions adverbially (lit. "light/quickly") qualifying the ensuing action. نزد شاهش = "before/near his king" (نزد = in the presence of/near; شاهش = his king). گشادند راه is a poetic word order: verb گشادند (opened) with راه (the ways/paths) — "the ways were opened." Together: "swiftly/in a light manner the ways to the king were opened."
segments.s072-g7: چو = "when." نزدیک شاه اندر آمد = "he came near the king/entered the king's presence" (نزدیک + شاه + اندر آمد). The final noun زمین here is held syntactically to the next verb for metrical/poetic ordering: it will be the object of the following verb (i.e. he kisses the earth).
segments.s072-g8: ببوسید is past "he kissed" with زمین (the earth/ground) as its object (moved into the previous segment for meter). و links the actions. بر شاه کرد آفرین literally "he made/uttered 'afarin' upon the king" — i.e. he praised/acclaimed the king; کرد is simple past "did/made," and آفرین is an acclamation ("bravo/praise").
segments.s072-g17: This is a prepositional phrase plus reported speech. به فر تو = 'to/for the farr of you' (به + فر + enclitic pronoun تو) with the preposition before the noun; گفتا is the archaic past-tense 3rd-person singular 'he said' (گفت + -ا ending common in epic Persian). همه بهتریست is a small clause: همه here functions adverbially/collectively ('altogether/entirely') and بهتریست is a stative/predicative form 'it is better' (archaic copula/formation).
segments.s072-g18: ابا تو is a genitival phrase 'your ابا (reluctance/refusal)' where the noun precedes the pronoun (poetic order). همه رنج is the subject 'all/sum of suffering(s)', and رامشگریست is a present/stative predicate 'is the soothing/peace-making' (the -است copula fused in older style); the clause says that your reluctance functions as the pacification of all hardship.
segments.s072-g19: This is an SOV-style line with a postposed subject. ازو = 'from him/of him' marks the origin or sender; بستد is the past 3rd-person singular (بستن) 'he closed/fastened/sealed'; آن نامه is the direct object 'that letter'; and پهلوان at the end is the subject 'the champion/hero' (poetic order places the subject after the object and verb).
segments.s072-g20: بخندید is the past 3rd-person singular of خندیدن 'he laughed'; و شد links the next resultant state 'and he became'; شاد و روشن‌روان are two predicate adjectives 'happy and bright‑soul(ed)/cheerful' describing his appearance/feeling. The sequence indicates action followed by resulting mood.
segments.s072-g21: چو = 'when' introducing a temporal clause. بر خواند is a prefixed verb meaning 'read aloud/recited' (بر as a verbal prefix + خواند, past tense). پاسخ چنین داد باز literally 'gave back such a reply': پاسخ (object) + چنین (demonstrative 'thus/such') + داد (past 'gave') and باز functions as 'back/again' (here 'returned' the answer). Word order is object before verb, typical in Persian poetic style.
segments.s072-g22: که introduces the content/result clause 'that…'. رنجی uses the indefinite/indeterminate form (رنج + -ی) 'a grief/pain'; فزودی is a past form of فزودن 'it increased/grew'; به دل is the locative/experiencer 'in the heart'; بر دراز ('بر' + adjective دراز) functions adverbially to indicate the grief 'grew long / was prolonged' — an idiomatic, somewhat archaic phrasing meaning the pain became lengthened/deepened in his heart.
segments.s072-g23: ولیکن is the concessive 'but/yet'. بدین here is the fused demonstrative/prepositional 'by/with this' (بدین = بـ + این in older usage) modifying نامه; نامه دلپذیر = 'a pleasing/heart‑pleasing letter' (دلپذیر postmodifies نامه). The whole phrase functions as an adverbial/nominal modifier linking to the following relative clause.
segments.s072-g24: که introduces a relative clause 'which'. بنوشت here is an archaic past form 'he wrote' (نوشتن with a prefixed form in epic diction). با is instrumental 'with/by', درد دل is a frozen genitive 'pain of the heart' (emotional sorrow), and سام پیر is the agent in apposition 'Sam the old' (پیر = 'old' as epithet). The clause therefore says the letter was written by the elderly Sam with the pain of his heart.
segments.s072-g25: اگر چه functions as a concessive conjunction ('although'); مرا هست is an older Persian pattern meaning 'there is to me' = 'I have' (مرا = 'to me / me', هست = 'is'). ازین (از + این) introduces the noun دل دژم ('this sullen heart') as the thing possessed; word order places the possessor-clause before the verb as common in older Persian.
segments.s072-g26: برانم is a first‑person present/subjunctive-form verb meaning 'I drive away / banish' (expressing intent). که here introduces the purpose/result clause and functions as 'so that'; نندیشم is negative present-subjunctive 'I do not think / I may not think'. از introduces the object of thought 'about' (بیش و کم = 'more and less', i.e. 'gain and loss').
segments.s072-g27: Two verbs in sequence (بسازم برآرم) are first‑person present/subjunctive forms expressing the speaker's resolve: 'I will make/arrange' and 'I will bring about/fulfill'. همه کام تو is the object phrase 'all your desires' with the possessive pronoun تو following the noun (classical Persian word order).
segments.s072-g28: گر is a conditional 'if' (here introducing the proviso). اینست is the copula 'this is'; فرجام آرام تو is the noun phrase 'the calm/peaceful outcome of yours' with آرام qualifying فرجام and the possessive pronoun تو placed after the adjective phrase, a common classical ordering.
segments.s072-g29: تو is the vocative/topic 'you'. یک چند is an idiom meaning 'a short while / for a little while' (چند as a temporal quantifier). اندر + به شادی gives the locative phrase 'in/at the celebration', and به پای here idiomatically means 'be present / remain' at that place — together giving 'stay a little while at the feast.'
segments.s072-g30: که تا forms a purposive conjunction combination ('so that / in order that'). من is the subject 'I'; به کارت is a prepositional phrase 'concerning your affairs' (به + کارت). زنم is first‑person present/subjunctive of زدن here meaning 'to apply/offer' (i.e. give counsel), and نیک رای is the object 'good counsel' (نیک qualifying رای).
segments.s072-g31: ببردند is a past (perfective) third‑person plural form 'they took / carried away'. خوالیگران is an obscure agent noun (here the acting attendants/servers); خوان زر is a noun phrase 'golden table/banquet' (خوان 'feast/table' followed by زر 'gold', a noun–noun/attributive compound common in poetry meaning 'golden banquet').
segments.s072-g32: شهنشاه is the title 'Shahanshah / king of kings' (subject). بنشست is past third‑person 'sat down'. با introduces accompaniment 'with', and زال زر is a name plus epithet literally 'Zal (in) gold' — either 'Zal adorned with gold' or 'Zal, the golden', the postnominal زر serving as an epithet.
segments.s072-g9: زمانی is a temporal adverb ('for a while'). همی داشت is a periphrastic imperfect/continuous construction ('was keeping / kept on') with همی marking continuous or habitual action; the verb precedes the following prepositional phrase. بر خاک روی is a prepositional phrase meaning 'with (his) face on the dust/ground' (بر + object + روی where روی = 'face'). Together the segment says someone remained prostrate for a time.
segments.s072-g10: This clause is inverted poetic order: بدو 'to him' (dative) appears first, داد is the past tense verb 'gave', دل is the direct object 'heart', and شاه آزرمجوی is the agent phrase 'the honor-seeking king' (شاه modified by the epithet آزرمجوی). So the king is the giver and the recipient is the person referred to by بدو.
segments.s072-g11: بفرمود is an honorific/polite past tense verb 'he commanded/ordered'. تا introduces a purpose/subordinate clause ('so that / in order that'). رویش is 'his face' (روی + enclitic ش = his). از خاک خشک literally 'from (the) dry dust' — here marking what is to be removed from the face. The verb that completes the clause follows in the next segment.
segments.s072-g12: ستردند is a past-plural verb 'they removed / they wiped off' (attendants implied). و links the next action: بر وی 'upon him' (on his person) and پراگند مشك = 'scattered musk' (پراگند 'scattered' modifying مشک 'musk'). The two actions are coordinated: they removed the dust and scattered/sprinkled musk on him.
segments.s072-g13: بیامد is simple past 'came'. بر تخت is a locative/prepositional phrase 'to/on the throne', and شاه ارجمند is the noun plus epithet 'the noble/esteemed king' (epithet follows the noun). The subject 'he' is implicit in the verb بیامد.
segments.s072-g14: بپرسید is past tense 'asked' (3rd person). ازو here indicates the person at whom the question was directed ('of/from him' — i.e., 'asked him'), while شهریار بلند is the agent/subject 'the lofty/high king' who does the asking; Persian often allows this inverted order in poetry.
segments.s072-g15: که introduces the direct question/relative sense here; چون بودی is the interrogative 'how were you' (چون = 'how', بودی = past of 'to be'). ای is the vocative 'O', and پهلو راد مرد is a descriptive vocative phrase 'man of generous flank' (پهلو 'flank/side' + راد 'generous') — together an honorific epithet addressing the addressee as a generous/valiant man.
segments.s072-g16: بدین is a prepositional phrase 'on/at this' (ب = to/at + دین as the remainder of the demonstrative in the manuscript); راه دشوار means 'the difficult road' (راه = road, دشوار = difficult). با باد و گرد 'with wind and dust' specifies the hardships encountered. The whole segment situates the question in a harsh, dusty journey.
segments.s072-g41: بیامد is a simple past, 3rd-person singular (‘he/it came’) with the subject omitted as usual in Persian. به شبگیر is an adverbial phrase meaning ‘toward night/at nightfall’ (به + an adjective/noun), and بسته کمر is a participial/adjectival phrase ‘girt at the waist’ (بسته = ‘bound/girt’, followed by کمر ‘waist/belt’) describing the same implied subject. The sequence places the verb first and the descriptive phrase after it — a common poetic order.
segments.s072-g42: به پیش is a prepositional phrase meaning ‘into the presence of / before’ (به = ‘to/towards’, پیش = ‘in the presence of’). منوچهر is the name of the king and پیروزگر is an epithet ‘the conqueror / victorious one’, placed after the name as a title. The whole phrase marks the destination/recipient of the arrival in the previous segment.
segments.s072-g43: برو is the imperative ‘Go!’ and here functions as an uttered command/interjection placed before the rest of the clause. آفرین کرد is a light-nominal plus auxiliary construction meaning ‘gave praise / applauded’ (آفرین = acclamation, کرد = did/made), and شاه جهان (‘the king of the world’) is the agent placed after the verb — a poetic subject–verb inversion.
segments.s072-g44: چو is the temporal conjunction ‘when’; برگشت is the past tense ‘he returned’. بستودش is the past form of ستودن with the enclitic object -ش (‘he praised him’); note the archaic/poetic form بستود (instead of ستود) plus the pronoun. اندر نهان is an old-fashioned prepositional phrase meaning ‘in secret’ (اندر = in/within, نهان = hidden/secret).
segments.s072-g33: بفرمود is a third-person past honorific/performative 'he commanded/said'. تا introduces a subordinate clause meaning 'that'/'so that' (not temporal here). نامداران همه is a plural noun phrase 'the renowned ones, all of them' with همه following to mean 'all/everyone'. Together: 'He commanded that all the renowned men ...'.
segments.s072-g34: نشستند is the 3rd-person plural past tense 'they sat' / 'they took their seats' and is the main verb of the subordinate clause introduced by تا. بر خوان شاه رمه is a prepositional noun phrase 'at/on the king's banquet/table'; خوان 'banquet', شاه 'king' as possessor, and رمه is an appositive/epithet meaning 'throng/multitude' attached to the banquet. Word order shows the preposition بر governing the whole phrase.
segments.s072-g35: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. از خوان خسرو is a prepositional phrase 'from Khosrow's table' (از + noun + possessor). بپرداختند is a past 3rd-person-plural verb here meaning 'they rose/they departed' (classical usage 'پرداختن' = to set off/leave); it is the verb of the temporal clause.
segments.s072-g36: به تخت دگر جای می‌ساختند: به + noun phrase marks direction/place 'to/at the throne (تخت) other (دگر)'. جای می‌ساختند is the verb phrase 'they made/they set places' (می‌ساختند is past-continuous/narrative plural). The order places the destination (به تخت دگر) before the object 'جای' which is then governed by the verb 'می‌ساختند'.
segments.s072-g37: چو is 'when' (temporal). می‌خورده شد is a classical compound expressing a resultant past: می‌خورده (imperfective participial sense of 'eat') + شد as auxiliary/resultive, here meaning 'had eaten / was finished eating'. نامور پور سام is the noun phrase 'the renowned son (پور) Sam' (a patrimonial name construction).
segments.s072-g38: نشست از بر اسپ زرین is a verb with a prepositional complement: نشست (here 'alighted / sat down off') + از بر + اسپ زرین 'from upon the golden steed' — a classical phrasing meaning he dismounted. ستام is uncertain in this line (it may be a proper name or an obscure epithet); its form in the provided senses suggests a verbal root but context favors a name/epithet, so its exact lexical sense is unclear.
segments.s072-g39: برفت is a classical past 3rd-person singular 'he went/left'. و 'and' links the verbs. بپیمود is a past verb 'he traversed / spanned / accomplished'. بالای شب is a noun phrase 'the top/height of the night' (بالای as 'above/at the top of' + شب 'night'), so the verbs describe movement through or over the night.
segments.s072-g40: This is a compressed, verbless descriptive couplet: پر اندیشه دل = 'the heart full of thought' where پر means 'full (of)' and links to اندیشه 'thought' modifying دل 'heart'. Parallel: پر ز گفتار لب = 'the lip full of speech', where ز (poetic 'از') marks source/contents ('full of speech'). Word order is adjective/quantifier + noun + head noun (positional, poetic syntax).

sentences.s072-s1: Then word reached the king that Sam the rider had come along Zal's road.
sentences.s072-s2: All the chiefs received him — those who bore rank in the kingdom.
sentences.s072-s3: When he drew near the palace, the approaches to the king were opened wide.
sentences.s072-s4: When he came near the king, he kissed the ground and cried 'bravo' to the king.
sentences.s072-s9: To your farr he said: “All is better; your reluctance is the soothing of all sorrow.”
sentences.s072-s10: The champion folded (closed) that letter from him,
sentences.s072-s11: When he read it aloud, he returned such an answer that a grief increased and lengthened in the heart.
sentences.s072-s12: But by this pleasing letter, which the old Sam wrote with the pain of his heart,
sentences.s072-s13: Although I have this sullen heart, I will drive it away so that I do not think about gain and loss.
sentences.s072-s14: I will fashion and bring about all your desires, if this is the peaceful outcome for you.
sentences.s072-s15: You, stay for a short while at the celebration, so that I may offer good counsel for your affairs.
sentences.s072-s16: The attendants removed the golden banquet; the Shahanshah sat down with Zal, adorned in gold.
sentences.s072-s5: For a while he kept his face on the ground; the king, seeking honour, gave him his heart.
sentences.s072-s6: He commanded that they brush the dry dust from his face and sprinkle musk upon him.
sentences.s072-s7: He came to the throne of the noble king; the lofty sovereign asked him.
sentences.s072-s8: ‘How were you, O generous man, on this difficult road with wind and dust?’
sentences.s072-s21: He came at nightfall, his waist girded, into the presence of Manuchehr the Conqueror.
sentences.s072-s22: "Go!" the king of the world exclaimed in praise; when he returned, he praised him secretly.
sentences.s072-s17: He commanded that all the renowned men take their seats at the king's banquet.
sentences.s072-s18: When they rose from Khosrow's table, they set places on another throne.
sentences.s072-s19: When the famed son of Sam had eaten, he alighted from the golden steed.
sentences.s072-s20: He departed and traversed the height of the night; his heart full of thought, his lips full of speech.
